header.stance: Front-end-led Treasury relief, with long-end inflation and supply premiums intact
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The July 14 Treasury close reversed Monday’s policy-led selloff, but it did not end the restrictive-rate regime. A broad June inflation slowdown removed much of the urgency around a July Federal Reserve increase and produced a front-end-led rally: the two-year yield closed at 4.18% and the 10-year at 4.58%. The curve bull-steepened because the immediate policy premium fell faster than the compensation embedded in long maturity. That outcome changed the prior close’s central interpretation from an imminent-tightening scare to a temporary extension of the Fed’s waiting period.

The inflation composition gave the rally substance. Headline CPI fell 0.4% in June and core CPI was unchanged, with cooling evident in shelter, energy, core goods and a services measure that excludes housing and energy. The release therefore did more than reverse a gasoline-driven headline surge. It weakened the case that broad underlying pressure required immediate action and pulled down real yields as well as inflation compensation. Money markets pushed the likely timing of any increase toward September or October after having assigned substantial weight to July before the release.

Relief remained bounded by two forces. Chair Kevin Warsh used congressional testimony to keep rate increases among the Fed’s available tools and argued that one benign month did not complete the inflation task. Renewed US-Iran fighting simultaneously lifted crude and refined fuels, meaning June CPI did not capture the latest energy impulse. Those counterforces explain why the early two-year rally was partly pared and why the 30-year yield fell much less than the front end. The close reduced near-term policy risk without granting a durable all-clear on inflation or term premium.

Market absorption was orderly but selective. A large Goldman Sachs bond sale drew deep demand across maturities, the six-week Treasury bill auction cleared with solid coverage, and listed credit returns rose. Yet a postponed data-center bond exposed saturation in a heavily financed AI segment, while high real yields and global sovereign supply kept the hurdle for duration elevated. Strong JGB auction demand and prospective domestic buyer support eased one international pressure point, though UK fiscal anxiety, Chinese government issuance and distressed sovereign cases showed that the cost and availability of funding still depended on borrower quality and buyer depth.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasuries rallied across the nominal curve, with the gains diminishing by maturity. The two-year yield fell eight basis points to 4.18%, the five-year declined six to 4.31%, the 10-year eased four to 4.58%, and the 30-year slipped two to 5.08%. The 2s10s slope widened four basis points to 40, while 2s30s widened six to 90. This was a clean bull steepening: the sector most exposed to the next Fed meetings led, and the long end retained more of its prior inflation, fiscal and supply premium.

The daily rally repaired only part of the accumulated cheapening. Over one week, the two-year was one basis point lower, while five-, 10- and 30-year yields remained four, three and three basis points higher. One-month changes were still positive across the curve, ranging from nine basis points at two years to 11 at 30 years. July 14 therefore interrupted the rise in yields without returning duration to its mid-June state. The persistence of the monthly move is important context for why long-end relief remained limited.

June CPI supplied the decisive near-term catalyst. Headline prices fell 0.4% from May after rising 0.5% in May, and the year-over-year rate slowed to 3.5% from 4.2%. Core prices were flat on the month and rose 2.6% over the year, down from 2.9%. Real average hourly earnings increased 0.1% year over year. The breadth of the monthly cooling gave policymakers room to wait even though annual inflation remained above target.

Energy fell 5.7% on the month, led by a 9.7% gasoline decline, but disinflation extended beyond fuel. Shelter rose just 0.1%, its smallest monthly increase since January 2021; owners’ equivalent rent increased 0.2%, rent 0.1%, and lodging fell 2.3%. Motor-vehicle insurance declined 2.0%, communication 1.5%, apparel 0.6%, and used vehicles 0.2%. A services measure excluding housing and energy fell 0.2%, matching its largest decline since the pandemic began. This breadth weakened the claim that one volatile category alone produced the benign reading.

The annual figures still imposed a limit. Energy was 15.7% higher than a year earlier, food rose 3.0%, shelter 3.3%, and airline fares 26.5%. Computer software and accessories prices also jumped 2.3% in June and 17.4% over the year. These pockets preserved a route from supply constraints, AI-related demand and renewed transport costs into future inflation. The bond rally consequently priced more time for the Fed; it did not price a completed return to price stability.

Fed pricing moved sharply around that distinction. The market-implied chance of a July increase fell below 20% from roughly 40% after the release, and futures shifted the more plausible timing toward September or October. The two-year yield traded as low as 4.14% intraday before giving back part of a 14-basis-point decline. Leveraged funds had entered the session with their largest short stance since September, so the inflation surprise likely combined a fundamental policy repricing with position adjustment.

Warsh prevented the move from becoming an unconditional easing signal. During three hours of testimony, he said June inflation was better than expected but kept interest rates within the tools that could be deployed if high inflation persisted. He stopped short of signaling a near-term increase, and some economists judged that he had offered no new guidance. The market response captured both messages: July tightening became remote, yet the rally faded as traders preserved a later-year tightening distribution.

Inflation-linked Treasuries showed that the benchmark rally was primarily a real-rate move. The 10-year real yield fell three basis points to 2.33%, while the 10-year breakeven declined one basis point to 2.25%. Five- and seven-year real yields also fell three basis points, to 2.03% and 2.17%; 20- and 30-year real yields eased two basis points to 2.67% and 2.87%. Lower expected policy restraint did most of the work, but the small breakeven decline showed that the CPI composition also reduced inflation compensation.

The real-yield curve retained a substantial long-horizon burden. Ten-year real yields were still three basis points higher over one week and 16 higher over one month; the 30-year real yield was three and 14 basis points higher over those horizons. A soft monthly CPI release can remove an immediate hike premium without resolving the return required for fiscal supply, resilient activity and large private investment programs. The nominal curve’s steepening and the real curve’s elevated levels expressed that separation.

Bills divided immediate cash demand from the outer policy horizon. Four- and eight-week coupon-equivalent yields were unchanged at 3.70% and 3.72%, while the six-week rose two basis points to 3.72%. Thirteen- and 17-week yields each fell five basis points, the 26-week declined eight, and the 52-week dropped 10 to 4.02%. The larger fall from three months onward aligned with reduced tightening expectations; stability at the shortest maturities kept the move from looking like a general funding dislocation.

The Treasury’s $95 billion 42-day bill auction stopped at a 3.640% high rate with 2.84 bid-to-cover and 63.10% allotted at the high rate. Accepted competitive tenders totaled $91.560 billion, including $66.253 billion for indirect bidders, $23.744 billion for primary dealers and $1.563 billion for direct bidders. The bill settles July 16 and matures August 27. The result established adequate six-week cash demand, though the lack of a supplied pre-auction reference makes the coverage and allocation details more informative than any price comparison.

Small-business evidence complicated the benign national inflation picture without overturning it. NFIB optimism rose 2.1 points to 97.4, close to its long-run average, as expectations for business conditions and real sales improved. A net 38% of owners raised prices, the highest since January 2023, even as the share planning increases fell two points to 32%. Current price setting remained firm, but softer intentions and better growth expectations fit a slower, not collapsing, inflation-growth mix.

Michael Barr’s AI remarks added a structural real-rate question beyond the session’s policy repricing. He saw little economy-wide job displacement but noted harder entry for some young workers and large productivity gains in controlled settings. Adoption was far more common among workers with graduate degrees than among those with no education beyond high school. AI could expand potential supply if access broadens, yet concentrated adoption and debt-funded infrastructure demand can distribute the gains unevenly and keep the neutral-rate debate open.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Listed credit participated in the Treasury and equity rally without showing a large quality split. LQD gained 0.23%, HYG rose 0.20%, and JNK added 0.15%. Those total returns reflected a combination of lower government yields and benign risk tone. The more useful credit message came from primary execution: high-grade borrowers could place substantial duration, while a more saturated AI-linked high-yield segment required sharper issuer and structure differentiation.

Goldman Sachs demonstrated the depth available to a profitable, frequent bank issuer. It raised $10 billion in three tranches spanning six to 31 years after reporting record equity-trading results and a 32% annual increase in fixed-income, currency and commodity revenue. Peak orders reached about $32 billion. The longest tranche priced at 113 basis points over Treasuries, 22 basis points tighter than initial talk, indicating that the rally translated into meaningful price compression during syndication.

The sale brought Goldman’s year-to-date dollar investment-grade issuance to $44 billion and formed most of the $13.9 billion sold across three US high-grade offerings on Tuesday. Strong absorption across a maturity range extending beyond 30 years showed that lower Treasury yields could mobilize demand even with oil rising and technology borrowers already supplying the market heavily. It did not remove the broader duration burden, but it showed that concession, earnings strength and a familiar bank capital structure still cleared size.

Prime Data Centers marked the other edge of access. The company initially proposed a $600 million Norwegian-law bond, reduced the expected size to $500 million and then postponed the transaction after some investors rejected the offered terms. Arctic Securities, the sole manager, cited deteriorating market conditions and left open a rapid reopening or a delay until after summer. The sequence showed that access was conditional even in a sector backed by strong secular demand.

Data-center developers had raised roughly $30 billion in the US high-yield market during the year, much of it supported by leases to large technology companies. Prime’s own Chicago subsidiary had completed an upsized $900 million sale in June for a fully leased project. The postponed parent financing indicates that prior execution and sponsor quality could not eliminate portfolio saturation, documentation concerns or tenant concentration. AI infrastructure remained financeable, but the marginal deal needed clearer protection and compensation.

Small-business borrowing supplied a quieter funding signal. Only 22% of surveyed firms borrowed regularly, 12 points below the historical average, while the average short-maturity loan rate fell 0.4 point to 7.4%, its lowest since October 2022. Lower borrowing costs were helpful, yet reduced regular use suggested that credit demand or willingness to add leverage remained restrained. Improving sales expectations therefore coexisted with a lending channel that was available but not accelerating broadly.

May cross-border flows, released at 4 p.m. ET, reinforced demand for longer US securities while revealing a different front-end pattern. The United States recorded a $132.2 billion net TIC inflow as $172.0 billion of private inflows more than offset $39.9 billion of official outflows. Foreign investors bought $262.8 billion of long-term US securities and a net $56.6 billion of Treasury notes and bonds. The release supported the idea that elevated long yields were attracting overseas capital, although it arrived late enough to describe the closing backdrop, not the origin of the morning rally.

The same release showed foreign Treasury-bill holdings falling $43.5 billion and US banks’ net dollar liabilities to foreign residents declining $70.0 billion. Long-duration demand and shorter-dollar balance-sheet contraction were therefore occurring together. Combined with the solid six-week auction, the figures point to selective cash allocation rather than a uniform withdrawal from dollar assets. Funding remained orderly, but the buyer base differed materially between longer securities, bills and bank liabilities.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Japan delivered the strongest developed-market duration relief. Twenty- and 30-year JGB yields fell as much as 18 basis points, to 3.565% and 3.725%, and the 40-year yield declined 10 basis points. The roughly ¥700 billion 20-year auction drew 4.52 times as many bids as awards, up from 2.97 at the previous sale, while the price tail narrowed to zero from 0.24. Actual auction demand gave the rally firmer footing than verbal support alone.

Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama also floated adding JGBs to tax-advantaged individual accounts and said the Government Pension Investment Fund could adjust its portfolio if needed. That reopened the buyer-base theme after reports had tempered expectations for a formal GPIF allocation overhaul. Estimates suggested the fund could add as much as ¥12.3 trillion of domestic government bonds within its existing range, but the speed and size of any shift remained decisive. Immediate JGB absorption eased comparative pressure on Treasuries; a lasting domestic reallocation could alter Japanese demand for foreign duration.

Oil and fiscal policy prevented a complete reset in Japan. Ambitious government spending, a weak yen and concern that Bank of Japan tightening was lagging inflation still burdened the super-long sector. The auction showed that concession could bring buyers back, yet it did not settle the path of official portfolio policy. For US duration, Japan moved from being a source of acute daily pressure to a conditional source of relief, with the longer-run cross-border effect still unresolved.

The UK carried a less favorable mix. Ahead of Andy Burnham’s move into Downing Street, internal forecasts were reported to have raised fourth-quarter inflation to 3.2%, projected higher gilt yields and expected at least one Bank of England increase by year-end. Higher borrowing costs had already reduced the government’s £23.6 billion fiscal-rule buffer by at least £5.5 billion. The possibility of looser policy under a new chancellor added a fiscal premium precisely as renewed Gulf disruption threatened energy and transport costs.

Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey supplied a structural growth counterpoint in a 4 p.m. ET speech. He said UK potential-supply growth had slowed from 2.8% before the financial crisis to 1.3% afterward, with productivity’s contribution dropping from 2.4 to 0.4 percentage point. His emphasis on resilient, proportionate bank regulation framed credit supply as part of the growth solution. Weak potential output lowers the economy’s speed limit, but a stronger banking channel can sustain lending without relying entirely on sovereign spending.

After the US fixed-income close, Chancellor Rachel Reeves added a capital-markets development that did not cause Tuesday’s Treasury move. The UK targeted its first digital sovereign bond by early 2027 and envisaged further issuance, alongside a pilot for tokenized deposits. Proposed bank-capital changes were estimated to enable as much as £150 billion of lending, and the expanded Growth Guarantee Scheme was designed to support £3.5 billion of annual small-business credit. The measures linked sovereign-market modernization to private credit creation, while leaving the near-term gilt fiscal premium intact.

China used central-bank liquidity to prepare for a heavy debt calendar. The People’s Bank of China announced a 1.4 trillion-yuan six-month outright reverse repo, which would provide a net 500 billion yuan after maturities. Together with an earlier three-month operation, July net additions through the tool would total 700 billion yuan. The operation was aimed at tax-payment pressure, credit expansion and debt demand, showing that sovereign issuance and money-market management were being treated as one transmission problem.

Estimates published after the US close put third-quarter central and local government net issuance at 4.2 trillion to 4.4 trillion yuan, above the prior year’s record for the period. First-half issuance had reached about 52% of the annual target, and local governments typically seek to complete sales by October. China’s 10-year yield had rebounded to around 1.74% from a 10-month low. More liquidity can smooth absorption, but record supply still raises the relative-yield hurdle across global bond markets.

European policy expectations remained exposed to the same energy shock. Traders had brought forward Bank of England and ECB tightening bets as Brent approached $90, and a Polish policymaker argued that a rate increase was more likely than a cut despite inflation at the central bank’s target. The US CPI surprise reduced the global force of an imminent Fed increase, but it could not directly resolve European imported-energy exposure or domestic fiscal constraints. Developed-market duration ended the day dispersed by buyer base, energy dependence and sovereign financing needs.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Emerging-market assets benefited from lower US yields and a softer dollar. EMB rose 0.20%, while the Dollar Index fell 0.34% to 100.94. Brazil’s real gained as much as 1.4%, and the South African rand and Mexican peso also advanced after CPI. The move eased the common discount-rate and currency burden for hard-currency borrowers. Rising oil and renewed Gulf attacks limited the rally later in the session, leaving energy exposure as the main divider beneath the broad improvement.

Indonesia illustrated that division. S&P’s affirmation of the sovereign rating offered support after an earlier bond selloff, but the 10-year domestic yield rose three basis points to 7.26% as investors focused on oil’s inflation effect. The government emphasized prudent and flexible issuance, yet a currency already pressured by domestic policy concerns remained vulnerable to imported fuel costs. Lower US front-end yields helped the external backdrop; they did not eliminate Indonesia’s local inflation and confidence premium.

Senegal remained a debt-management outlier. Some international bondholders began informal discussions about forming a creditor group as the government considered hiring an adviser. A 2024 discovery of $7 billion in previously unreported debt had lifted public debt to 132% of GDP and prompted the IMF to suspend a $1.8 billion program. Sovereign spreads exceeded 1,500 basis points, and demand for longer instruments in the regional market was eroding. The question had moved from ordinary issuance toward whether a reprofiling or broader restructuring would be needed.

The political and official-financing channels made Senegal’s case especially difficult. The authorities were seeking a new IMF facility, but domestic disagreement persisted over the form of any debt treatment. Heavy reliance on regional funding transmitted sovereign stress into local balance sheets even as global EM risk tone improved. Tuesday’s fall in the dollar could reduce the common external burden at the margin; disclosure credibility, program negotiations and creditor coordination remained the binding variables.

China’s liquidity action and prospective supply also mattered for EM dollar conditions through a different route. Large PBOC injections can stabilize domestic funding and support credit creation, yet record government borrowing competes for bank balance sheets and signals pressure on demand. The combination may keep Chinese rates contained while increasing the need for official liquidity. Across emerging markets, the session separated countries receiving cyclical relief from lower US rates from borrowers whose fiscal structure, energy imports or refinancing needs dominated the common move.

## Cross asset

US equities treated softer inflation as compatible with continued growth. The S&P 500 rose 0.38%, the Nasdaq 100 gained 1.10%, and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index advanced 2.54%. The VIX fell 3.85% to 16.50. Technology leadership and lower volatility reinforced the reduction in immediate Fed risk, while strong bank earnings helped explain why the Treasury rally occurred alongside risk appetite instead of a flight from growth.

Energy moved against the disinflation signal. WTI rose 1.54% to $79.34 and Brent gained 1.72% to $84.73 as renewed fighting disrupted Gulf flows; intraday reporting put oil above $87 and described even sharper rebounds in gasoline, diesel and jet fuel. President Donald Trump backed away from the proposed 20% cargo charge through Hormuz, easing one transport-cost threat, but attacks on tankers and continued strikes preserved the supply risk. June CPI captured the earlier gasoline decline, not this renewed impulse.

The dollar and gold aligned with lower rate expectations and residual geopolitical concern. The Dollar Index fell to 100.94, its weakest tone in almost two weeks, while gold rose 1.60% to $4,061.10. A lower real-yield path reduced the carry advantage of the dollar and the opportunity cost of bullion. Gold’s gain alongside equities and falling volatility also indicated that rate relief and event hedging could coexist without a broad liquidation into safety.

Credit funds rose modestly and EM debt participated, extending the benign cross-asset response. The pattern was coherent with an inflation surprise that delayed tightening while leaving growth intact. Oil was the clear counterforce, and the partial reversal of the early Treasury rally showed that markets did not ignore it. Cross assets therefore supported a narrower conclusion: immediate policy pressure eased, but the durability of that relief still depended on whether renewed fuel disruption reaches producer costs, household prices and Fed communication.

## Forward Market Significance

June PPI and the July Empire State survey at 8:30 a.m. ET on July 15 begin the next US reaction-function test, followed by Warsh’s Senate testimony at 10:00, Governor Lisa Cook’s outlook speech at 1:00 p.m. and the Beige Book at 2:00. Muted producer-price categories that feed core PCE, softer factory pricing and regional reports of slower price setting would confirm that June CPI reflects broad disinflation and could extend the decline in two- to five-year real yields. Firm pipeline costs, resilient orders or Fed language emphasizing an earlier tightening response would challenge the idea that July 14 created more than a one-meeting pause. The two-year yield, short-dated Fed pricing, the 10-year real yield and breakevens will distinguish a durable reaction-function shift from position-driven relief.

The EIA petroleum report at 10:30 a.m. ET on July 15, followed by June import and export prices at 8:30 a.m. on July 17 and preliminary Michigan inflation expectations at 10:00, isolates the energy pass-through that June CPI could not capture. Rebuilding crude and product inventories, stable refinery operation, contained trade prices and easing household expectations would resolve part of the conflict between falling June gasoline prices and Tuesday’s renewed oil advance, reinforcing lower breakevens and a patient Fed path. Further product draws, transport disruption, accelerating import prices or higher expectations would reopen the inflation shock seen at the prior close. Breakevens, the front end, the dollar and lower-quality credit are the sensitive channels because a persistent fuel impulse would combine higher policy rates with weaker household purchasing power.

The $72 billion 17-week bill auction at 11:30 a.m. ET on July 15, the July 16 settlement of Tuesday’s $95 billion 42-day bill, and the combined $210 billion four- and eight-week auctions at 11:30 on July 16 form the next cash-absorption sequence. Strong coverage, broad direct and indirect awards, stable secondary bill yields and orderly secured funding would confirm that the July 14 rally reflected policy repricing while money-market liquidity remained ample. Weak demand, heavy dealer awards or bill cheapening through settlement would challenge that separation and add a funding premium to the front end. Reception across the sequence will also clarify whether the six-week auction’s solid headline coverage represented durable cash demand as Treasury supply extends across adjacent maturities.

section_summaries.market_read: The close removed an immediate hike premium without erasing the long-end duration burden.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether pipeline inflation and Fed language validate the front-end repricing.
section_summaries.summary: Broad June disinflation turned the prior tightening scare into a temporary extension of the Fed's waiting period, producing a front-end-led rally and bull steepening. Renewed energy pressure, Warsh's retained tightening option, elevated real yields and selective primary-market access kept the relief conditional.

section_summaries.market_read: Real rates carried the rally, but curve steepening preserved fiscal and inflation term premium.
section_summaries.next_check: The next test is whether producer pricing and Fed guidance sustain lower short real yields.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Cooling across headline, core, shelter and core services drove a real-yield-led Treasury rally, while the front end outperformed and the curve steepened. Persistent annual inflation pockets, elevated long-horizon real yields and a partial intraday retracement limited the shift to a near-term policy delay.

section_summaries.market_read: Lower yields supported credit, yet issuer quality and deal structure remained decisive for access.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether the next marginal borrower clears without wider concessions or stronger protection.
section_summaries.summary: Credit funds joined the risk rally, but primary markets revealed sharper borrower differentiation. A large, profitable bank placed long-duration debt with deep demand, while a postponed data-center deal showed that sector saturation, structure and compensation still constrained marginal high-yield access.

section_summaries.market_read: Global duration pressure eased unevenly as buyer depth, energy exposure and fiscal supply diverged.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether Japanese domestic demand persists as UK and Chinese issuance pressures build.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Strong demand at Japan's super-long auction eased one source of global duration pressure, but durable relief still depends on domestic buyer reallocation. UK fiscal anxiety, European energy exposure and China's record sovereign supply kept global term premiums tied to local funding capacity.

section_summaries.market_read: Common rate relief helped EM, but energy, fiscal credibility and refinancing structure drove dispersion.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch oil-sensitive currencies and progress on Senegal's official financing and creditor talks.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: Lower US yields and a softer dollar eased the common burden on emerging-market debt, but country-specific constraints dominated the tails. Oil-import exposure weighed on Indonesia, while Senegal's hidden-debt legacy, suspended official support and creditor coordination needs kept its case beyond ordinary issuance risk.

section_summaries.market_read: Risk assets confirmed policy relief, while oil prevented a clean disinflationary cross-asset signal.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch whether fuel disruption reaches expectations, the dollar and lower-quality credit.
section_summaries.summary: Softer inflation supported equities, technology leadership, lower volatility, a weaker dollar, stronger gold and modest credit gains, consistent with delayed tightening rather than a growth shock. Rising oil was the clear counterforce, preserving an inflation channel that helped pare the early Treasury rally.

section_summaries.market_read: The next sessions can confirm policy repricing or expose energy and funding limits to the rally.
section_summaries.next_check: Track front-end rates, breakevens and bill reception across the specified release sequence.
section_summaries.summary: Three tests will determine whether the rally was durable: producer prices and Fed communication, energy pass-through into expectations, and cash absorption across upcoming bill supply. Their outcomes should separate a genuine reaction-function shift from position relief or a new funding premium.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.33% (-3 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.25% (-1 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 40 bp (+4 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 90 bp (+6 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Treasuries bull-steepened as broad CPI cooling reduced immediate Fed tightening risk
overall_summary.session_narrative: The completed NY close showed a broad Treasury rally led by maturities most exposed to upcoming Fed meetings. Cooling across shelter, goods and core services cut the urgency of a July increase and lowered real yields, while risk assets and listed credit also advanced. The move stopped short of an all-clear because renewed Gulf disruption lifted energy costs, Warsh preserved later-year tightening optionality, and long-end supply premiums remained elevated.
overall_summary.summary: Broad June disinflation extended the Fed's waiting period and pulled real yields lower, with the front end leading the rally. Oil's renewed rise, later-year tightening risk and persistent fiscal and supply premiums kept long-duration relief limited.

watch_events.market_implication: Confirmation should extend front-end and real-yield relief; firmer costs would restore hike risk.
watch_events.market_trigger: Muted PCE-feeding categories and softer price setting would validate a longer Fed wait.
watch_events.title: US inflation pipeline and Fed response
watch_events.watch_line: PPI, factory pricing and Fed remarks will test whether June's disinflation is broad.

watch_events.market_implication: A persistent shock would lift breakevens and front-end risk while pressuring weaker credit.
watch_events.market_trigger: Inventory draws, transport disruption or firmer import prices would prolong the oil impulse.
watch_events.title: Energy pass-through into inflation
watch_events.watch_line: Petroleum balances, trade prices and household expectations will test fuel pass-through.

watch_events.market_implication: That outcome would add a funding premium to the front end despite lower policy risk.
watch_events.market_trigger: Weak coverage, heavy dealer awards or secondary cheapening would signal thinner absorption.
watch_events.title: Treasury bill absorption sequence
watch_events.watch_line: Bill auctions and settlement will test whether cash demand remains broad and funding orderly.
